Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex [The HomeLand Justice group, with basic skill levels, targets water and wastewater systems. They employ a multi-stage complex attack involving reconnaissance, resource development, initial access via phishing, execution through application exploitation, persistence with web shells, privilege escalation abusing elevation controls, defense evasion disabling security tools, credential access through brute forcing, discovery mapping networks, lateral movement using alternate authentication materials, collection of local system data, exfiltration over command and control channels, and impact through disrupting inter-process communication.]
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Network Traffic, Script File, Windows Registry, User Account, Target Artifact (Mobile device), Application Log, File System, Target Artifact (Channel)
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Delivery Method, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Web Shell, Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism, Disabling Security Tools, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Network Service Scanning, Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers conduct active scanning to identify potential vulnerabilities in the water and wastewater systems' public-facing infrastructure.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers establish proxy servers and email accounts to mask their operations and avoid direct tracing.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2021-42237 (Suggested): Sitcore XP contains an insecure deserialization vulnerability which can allow for remote code execution.
stage.description: Attackers send phishing emails to employees with login credentials or links to compromised websites mimicking official portals.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2023-50224 (Suggested): TP-Link TL-WR841N contains an authentication bypass by spoofing vulnerability within the httpd service.
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Attackers exploit known vulnerabilities in web applications used by the water system to execute unauthorized commands.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: Attackers install a backdoor on a server to maintain persistent access post-compromise.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Attackers escalate privileges within the network to gain higher-level permissions.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: Attackers turn off antivirus software and logging mechanisms to evade detection and cover their tracks.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 49 Name: Password Brute Forcing
stage.description: Attackers attempt to gain valid credentials through repeated login attempts.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Attackers map out the internal network structure to identify valuable assets.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Using stolen credentials, attackers move laterally across the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 328 Name: TCP 'RST' Flag Checksum Probe
stage.description: Attackers gather sensitive data related to operational technology (OT) systems.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Collected data is transferred to attacker-controlled servers.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276 Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Attackers disrupt control systems to simulate a wiper malware effect, causing operational failure.

Scenario.summary: The HomeLand Justice group employs a sophisticated, multi-stage attack targeting water and wastewater systems. Starting with reconnaissance, they identify vulnerabilities, develop resources, and gain initial access through phishing. Execution follows with exploitation of public-facing applications. Persistence is achieved through web shells, while privilege escalation and defense evasion techniques are employed to maintain control. Credential access, discovery, lateral movement, and data collection lead to exfiltration of sensitive data, culminating in impactful disruptions to critical operations.
